title: FAQ
orientation: A grouped FAQ from Abolitionists Rising answering common questions from pro-life readers, pro-choice readers, lawmakers, and people curious about the movement itself. It serves as the movement's standard reference for objections, definitions, and practical guidance.

questions.question: Do abolitionists support IVF?
questions.answer: No. Common IVF practice involves fertilizing extra embryos, discarding lower-grade ones, and selectively reducing pregnancies, which abolitionists view as wicked. They argue that creating life belongs to the marriage of husband and wife, not to scientists in a lab.
questions.quote: Such practices are self-evidently wicked. Though some believe there is an ethical way to practice IVF, we believe that the creation of life should not be done by scientists in a lab, but rather through the God-ordained method for creating life: intercourse of husband and wife.

questions.question: Is hormonal birth control compatible with the abolitionist position?
questions.answer: Barrier methods are acceptable because they only block sperm from reaching the egg. Hormonal birth control, however, can thin the uterine lining so that a newly conceived zygote cannot implant, which would induce an early abortion.
questions.quote: All forms of hormonal birth control, on the other hand, pose a risk of causing an early abortion.

questions.question: Can non-Christians join the Abolitionist Movement?
questions.answer: No. Abolitionism is explicitly Christian, and three of its five tenets (Gospel-Centered, Biblical, Body-driven) cannot be coherently held by non-Christians. Anti-abortion unbelievers are seen as a mission field rather than partners in the movement.
questions.quote: And since the Abolitionist Movement is an explicitly Christian movement, it is impossible for us to partner with non-Christians, or for non-Christians to join the movement.

questions.question: How did the Abolitionist Movement get started?
questions.answer: In 2011, T. Russell Hunter was researching pro-life groups for his church while simultaneously studying the historical abolitionists of slavery for his doctoral work. He noticed the same compromises that delayed the abolition of slavery were being repeated by the Pro-Life Movement, prompting him to call for a separate, uncompromising movement grounded in Scripture.
questions.quote: In short order, it became clear that many of the very practices and strategies that the abolitionists blamed for the delay of the abolition of slavery were being practices and strategies being employed by the Pro-Life Movement.

questions.question: Why not argue against abortion from a secular perspective so non-Christians can join?
questions.answer: Abolitionists hold that only God's revelation provides an objective standard of morality, so secular arguments lack a real foundation. They point to William Lloyd Garrison and insist the Holy Spirit, not clever argument, is what changes the hearts of abortion supporters.
questions.quote: God, the transcendent source of all morality, is the only standard by which evils like abortion can be adequately rebuked.

questions.question: Was the Dobbs decision a step in the right direction?
questions.answer: Abolitionists say no. They argue Dobbs implicitly denied preborn personhood by handing abortion policy back to states, when the Constitution already required equal protection for preborn children. They also note states were always free to ignore Roe via nullification clauses.
questions.quote: But in order to say that the States and Congress can write whatever abortion policy they want, you have to deny that the preborn child is a person.

questions.question: Why call yourselves abolitionists instead of pro-life?
questions.answer: Abolitionists say pro-life leaders have actively opposed abolition bills and hold heterodox positions like supporting impunity for aborting mothers. Linguistic differentiation is necessary to keep the categories clear, especially because pro-life groups often co-opt abolitionist language.
questions.quote: The pro-life groups know that differentiation between us and them is key to the growth of the Abolitionist Movement and that's why they often steal our language and pass themselves off as abolitionists.

questions.question: Can we end abortion without prosecuting mothers?
questions.answer: No. If a mother faces no possibility of legal penalty for procuring an abortion, then abortion is still legal in practice. Self-managed abortion would simply continue under any law that immunizes the mother.
questions.quote: If someone is legally permitted to commit an act without any possibility of legal penalty, then that act is legal.

questions.question: Is it really scientific to say life begins at fertilization?
questions.answer: Yes. The article cites multiple embryology textbooks (Moore, Larsen, Langman, O'Rahilly, Carlson, and others) all stating that a new human being begins at fertilization. Embryologist C. Ward Kischer says it is scientific fact, not belief.
questions.quote: Every human embryologist in the world knows that the life of the new individual human being begins at fertilization. It is not belief. It is scientific fact.

questions.question: Won't criminalizing abortion just push women into unsafe back-alley procedures?
questions.answer: Abolitionists say criminalization would dramatically reduce abortions because most people will not risk murder charges. Some will still break the law, just as some still murder born people, but that is the lawbreaker's fault, not the law's.
questions.quote: If abortion is criminalized as murder, there will be far fewer abortions.

questions.question: What about ectopic pregnancies, where the mother's life is in danger?
questions.answer: Most ectopic babies are already deceased when diagnosed, and removing a deceased baby is not abortion. When the baby is still alive, abolitionists oppose surgery or methotrexate that kills the child and instead favor watchful waiting plus an attempted transplant of the baby into the uterus if the mother's condition worsens.
questions.quote: Ultimately, our position is that the ethical treatment of an ectopic pregnancy in the case of a baby who is still alive involves watchful waiting/expectant management or an attempted transplant of the baby into the uterus.

questions.question: Isn't it true that you can't legislate morality?
questions.answer: Abolitionists argue the opposite: every law legislates someone's morality, from speed limits to child-support statutes. The real question is whose morality gets enshrined in law.

questions.question: Isn't outlawing abortion the same as forcing women to donate their organs?
questions.answer: No, for four reasons: organ donation is extraordinary care while not killing your child is ordinary; parents create the child's vulnerable position and owe greater care; the uterus exists for gestating offspring just as breasts exist for nursing; and refusing to donate an organ is not equivalent to actively dismembering a baby.
questions.quote: Outlawing abortion is not the same thing as forced organ donation for four reasons.

questions.question: What about pregnancies caused by rape or incest?
questions.answer: Abolitionists view rape as a heinous crime deserving capital punishment and full community support for victims. But they hold that murdering an innocent child for the sins of the father is itself evil, and aborting babies conceived in incest because of disability risk is plain eugenics.
questions.quote: But it is insane and evil to murder a child for the sins of the father.

questions.question: Would an abolition law outlaw treatment for miscarriages?
questions.answer: No. Miscarriage and abortion are different things, and no abolition bill ever written has banned removing a deceased fetus. OK SB1729 explicitly excludes spontaneous miscarriage from the bill's scope.
questions.quote: No abortion ban ever written would outlaw the removing of a deceased fetus from the uterus.

questions.question: Under an abolition law, would every aborting mother be charged with first-degree murder?
questions.answer: No. Abolition bills make preborn children equal under the law, so all the standard criminal-law immunities, justifications, and mitigating factors still apply. Charges would range from first-degree murder to manslaughter, or no charges at all in cases such as coercion.
questions.quote: An abolition bill simply makes preborn children equal under law.

questions.question: If I had an abortion before an abolition law passed, could I be prosecuted?
questions.answer: No. Abolition bills only apply prospectively, consistent with the U.S. constitutional ban on ex post facto prosecution. OK SB1729 explicitly limits the law to conduct after its effective date.
questions.quote: Every abolition bill applies only to crimes committed after the passage of the bill.

questions.question: Can the Abolitionists Rising team come to my campus or town?
questions.answer: Probably not, since they're a small team based in central Oklahoma. Instead they encourage readers to study their educational materials, flyer cars in public parking lots with abolitionist literature, and connect with Norman-Statement-affiliated churches via the state pages on their website.
questions.quote: We are a small team of people located in central Oklahoma, and mainly just travel close by or do day-long activism trips.

related.title: Norman Statement
related.reason: FAQ repeatedly references the Norman Statement as the doctrinal basis for the movement

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Expands the FAQ's argument for distinguishing abolitionism from the Pro-Life Movement

related.title: Biblical, Not Secular
related.reason: Develops the FAQ point that abolition must be argued from Scripture rather than secular reasoning

related.title: Criminalization
related.reason: Backs up the FAQ position that ending abortion requires prosecuting mothers

related.title: Immediatism
related.reason: One of the five tenets the FAQ cites when explaining who counts as an abolitionist

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: Supports the FAQ's claim that states could have ignored Roe via nullification clauses long before Dobbs

related.title: A Mother is a Magistrate: Why Duress Is No Defense for Abortion
related.reason: Goes deeper on the FAQ's case for prosecuting aborting mothers
